category.label: Systems of Equations
category.description: Solving and interpreting systems of linear and nonlinear equations and inequalities across two and three variables, using graphical, substitution, and elimination methods, including special-case and parameter analysis.
category.yield_rationale: Systems reappear in AP Precalc for function-model construction (1.14, 2.5, 2.14, 3.7) and in sinusoidal parameter extraction, but the category itself is a supporting prerequisite rather than a direct exam question pool.

umbrellas.label: System Solution Meaning and Notation
umbrellas.description: Core structure of simultaneous systems: what a solution means, ordered-pair and ordered-triple notation, verification, and the geometric interpretation of solutions as intersection points.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Foundational vocabulary — solution meaning and notation feed all downstream system work — but the concepts themselves appear infrequently as isolated exam questions once methods are established.

in_depth.label: System of equations structure
in_depth.description: Recognizes a system as two or more equations that must hold simultaneously, distinguishing a multi-equation system from a single equation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Purely definitional; tested only in early intro items. Downstream skills assume this knowledge.

in_depth.label: Simultaneous solution meaning
in_depth.description: A solution satisfies every equation in the system at the same time; values satisfying only one equation are not system solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual anchor needed for verification and special-case reasoning, but rarely the primary tested skill.

in_depth.label: Ordered-pair solutions to two-variable systems
in_depth.description: A two-variable system solution is written as an ordered pair (x, y) where coordinate order corresponds to variable assignment.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Notation skill; prerequisite for answer presentation in all system problems.

in_depth.label: Ordered-triple solutions to three-variable systems
in_depth.description: A three-variable system solution is written as an ordered triple (x, y, z); coordinate position matches the correct variable.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Notation skill for three-variable work; lower frequency than two-variable notation.

in_depth.label: Solution verification for two-variable systems
in_depth.description: Checks a proposed ordered pair by substituting into every equation in the system, confirming it satisfies all simultaneously.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Verification is exercised regularly after solving and appears as an explicit exam step in both MC and FRQ contexts.

in_depth.label: Solution verification for three-variable systems
in_depth.description: Checks a proposed ordered triple by substituting all three values into every equation in the system.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Same verification logic extended to three variables; less frequent than two-variable verification.

in_depth.label: System solution as intersection point
in_depth.description: Connects algebraic solutions to the geometric intersection of graphs; a system solution is the point (or set of points) where all equation graphs meet.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Algebraic-graphical connection is exercised throughout, especially in nonlinear-systems and graphical-methods work.

umbrellas.label: System Solution-Set Types and Classification
umbrellas.description: Classification of linear systems by solution-set type (consistent/independent, inconsistent, dependent), solution-set notation, and the role of variable and equation counts.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Classification vocabulary feeds special-case analysis and parameter problems; appears frequently in both method-based and structure-based question types.

in_depth.label: Consistent and independent system classification
in_depth.description: A consistent system has at least one solution; an independent linear system has exactly one solution because the equations represent distinct intersecting lines. Recognizing a system as consistent and independent is the default classification for standard two-variable linear systems.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Merged from consistent_systems and independent_linear_systems. Most standard system problems produce an independent system; consistent/independent together form one natural classification concept.

in_depth.label: Inconsistent and dependent system classification
in_depth.description: An inconsistent system has no solution (parallel lines in two-variable linear form); a dependent system has infinitely many solutions because the equations represent the same line or equivalent relationship. Both are identified by proportionality analysis of coefficients or by a false/true identity produced during solving.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Merged from inconsistent_systems and dependent_linear_systems. Detecting inconsistency and dependence are closely paired classification tasks tested together in the same question type; unifying them reflects the proportionality-vs-identity distinction as a single decision point.

in_depth.label: System solution-set notation
in_depth.description: Represents system solutions as ordered pairs, ordered triples, the empty set, or an infinity statement, depending on solution type.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Answer-format skill; tested primarily in intro-level problems; assumed in downstream work.

in_depth.label: Number of variables and equations in systems
in_depth.description: Recognizes system size (number of variables and equations) before selecting a solution method, since size affects possible solution behavior.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Structural recognition skill; background knowledge rather than a direct exam question target.

in_depth.label: Equivalent systems
in_depth.description: Equivalent systems share the same solution set; valid transformations (multiplying an equation by a nonzero constant, combining equations) produce equivalent systems without changing solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Underpins elimination and equation-scaling reasoning; important as a justification concept in FRQ explanations.

umbrellas.label: Graphical Systems — Plotting and Intersection
umbrellas.description: Plotting two-variable linear systems by applying slope-intercept or standard form, handling special lines (horizontal, vertical), and reading the intersection as the system solution.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Graphical plotting and intersection reading are the core representation skills for linear systems; feed the graphical connection to function zeros and solutions in AP Precalc.

in_depth.label: Graphical intersection of two lines
in_depth.description: Reads the graphical solution of a two-variable linear system as the ordered pair at the point where the two lines cross.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Core graphical skill exercised in every representation-based system question.

in_depth.label: Graphing systems from slope-intercept form
in_depth.description: Graphs a two-line system by applying each line's slope and y-intercept, then identifies the intersection as the solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slope-intercept graphing is the most natural form for quick line sketching in system contexts.

in_depth.label: Graphing systems from standard form
in_depth.description: Graphs a system whose equations are in ax + by = c form using intercepts or by converting to slope-intercept form before plotting.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Standard-form graphing is less intuitive; tested when students must convert form before graphing.

in_depth.label: Graphing systems with horizontal lines
in_depth.description: Handles systems containing y = c equations by graphing a horizontal line and reading the intersection with the other equation's graph.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Special structural case; tested as a recognition-and-intersection skill.

in_depth.label: Graphing systems with vertical lines
in_depth.description: Handles systems containing x = c equations by graphing a vertical line and reading the intersection with the other equation's graph.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Special structural case; less common than horizontal-line systems.

umbrellas.label: Graphical Systems — Interpretation and Special Cases
umbrellas.description: Interpreting graphical system results: parallel lines (no solution), coincident lines (infinitely many solutions), estimating non-grid intersections, and recognizing limits of graphical precision.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Graphical interpretation of special cases and precision limits reinforces algebraic classification and feeds AP Precalc representation questions.

in_depth.label: Parallel lines and no solution
in_depth.description: Identifies that two distinct lines with equal slopes but different y-intercepts never intersect, producing an inconsistent system with no solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slope-based inconsistency detection is a high-frequency classification task.

in_depth.label: Coincident lines and infinitely many solutions
in_depth.description: Identifies that two equations representing the same line (coincident lines) have every point in common, producing a dependent system with infinitely many solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Coincident-line recognition complements parallel-line recognition; together they cover all special graphical cases.

in_depth.label: Estimating system solutions from graphs
in_depth.description: Approximates an intersection point when it does not fall exactly on grid lines, distinguishing graphical estimation from exact algebraic solving.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Estimation from graphs appears in representation-based problems; less frequent than exact-intersection reading.

in_depth.label: Exact versus approximate system solutions
in_depth.description: Distinguishes between solutions that can be read exactly from a graph and those that require algebraic methods for precise values.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual distinction; most often appears as a justification or reasoning question.

in_depth.label: Graph scale and axis reading in system solutions
in_depth.description: Applies the coordinate scale and tick-mark spacing correctly when reading intersection points from a graph; guards against misreading non-unit scales.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Scale-reading errors are common in MC distractors but the concept itself is a supporting skill.

in_depth.label: Intersection outside the viewing window
in_depth.description: Recognizes that two non-parallel lines may intersect outside the visible graph window, so absence of a visible crossing does not imply no solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Edge case in graphical interpretation; low but nonzero frequency in MC reasoning items.

umbrellas.label: Substitution Method — Setup and Variants
umbrellas.description: Setting up the substitution method: choosing which variable to isolate, substituting explicit or isolated expressions, and handling negative, fraction, and decimal coefficient variants.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Substitution setup is the entry skill for the method most directly reused in AP Precalc function-model construction (solving for parameters from two conditions).

in_depth.label: Substitution with an explicit variable expression
in_depth.description: Applies substitution when one equation already gives a variable explicitly (e.g., y = 2x + 1), replacing that variable directly in the other equation without prior isolation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Most common substitution entry point; appears in the widest variety of system problems.

in_depth.label: Substitution after isolating a variable
in_depth.description: Isolates one variable in an equation before substituting into the other equation; required when neither equation is already solved for a variable.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Two-step substitution setup is a frequent MC skill; isolating first is the prerequisite move.

in_depth.label: Substitution into a standard-form equation
in_depth.description: Substitutes a solved expression into an equation in ax + by = c form, correctly distributing within coefficients.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Standard-form target equations are the most error-prone substitution destination; tested regularly.

in_depth.label: Substitution with negative expressions
in_depth.description: Substitutes a negative expression (e.g., y = -2x + 5) or substitutes into a negative-coefficient term, requiring correct sign handling and parentheses.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign errors in substitution are among the most common student mistakes; high distractor frequency.

in_depth.label: Substitution with fraction coefficients
in_depth.description: Applies substitution when equations involve fractional coefficients, requiring careful fraction arithmetic during and after replacement.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fraction coefficient systems appear regularly; arithmetic complexity increases question difficulty.

in_depth.label: Substitution with decimal coefficients
in_depth.description: Applies substitution when equations contain decimal coefficients, targeting decimal arithmetic inside the substitution step.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Decimal coefficient systems appear occasionally; less frequent than fraction variants.

umbrellas.label: Substitution Method — Execution and Special Cases
umbrellas.description: Completing the substitution method: solving the resulting one-variable equation, back-substitution to find the remaining variable, and detecting no-solution and dependent special cases.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Back-substitution and special-case detection are the completion steps of every substitution solution; they also gate understanding of system classification.

in_depth.label: One-variable equation formed by substitution
in_depth.description: Recognizes and solves the single-variable linear equation that results after substitution reduces the system.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Identifying and solving the resulting one-variable equation is the central procedural step of the substitution method.

in_depth.label: Back-substitution in two-variable systems
in_depth.description: After finding one variable's value, substitutes it back into an original equation to determine the remaining variable and complete the ordered pair.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Back-substitution is required in every complete substitution-method solution; a common site of incomplete-answer errors.

in_depth.label: No-solution case detected by substitution
in_depth.description: Identifies an inconsistent system when substitution produces a false numeric statement (e.g., 0 = 5), signaling no solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Special-case detection via substitution is an explicit exam question type; requires recognizing the false statement as a system-level result.

in_depth.label: Dependent case detected by substitution
in_depth.description: Identifies a dependent system when substitution produces a true identity (e.g., 0 = 0), signaling infinitely many solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Companion to no-solution detection; both special cases are tested together in classification problems.

in_depth.label: Choosing substitution when a coefficient is 1
in_depth.description: Recognizes that substitution is efficient when one variable has a coefficient of 1 or -1, because isolation avoids introducing fractions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Method-selection reasoning appears in higher-difficulty MC items; practical strategy knowledge.

umbrellas.label: Elimination Method — Setup and Scaling
umbrellas.description: Setting up elimination: choosing a variable to eliminate, adding or subtracting equations with opposite or matching coefficients, and scaling one or both equations to create cancellation.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Elimination setup is the primary method for standard-form systems and is directly exercised in solving 2x2 parameter systems for AP Precalc model construction.

in_depth.label: Elimination with opposite coefficients
in_depth.description: Adds two equations directly when one variable has equal-and-opposite coefficients, immediately canceling that variable without scaling.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The most direct elimination structure; appears in the widest range of elimination problems.

in_depth.label: Elimination with matching coefficients
in_depth.description: Subtracts one equation from another when a variable has identical coefficients in both equations, removing that variable.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Subtraction-based elimination requires sign discipline; frequently tested alongside addition-based elimination.

in_depth.label: Elimination by adding equations
in_depth.description: Adds the two equations to eliminate a variable whose coefficients are additive inverses, producing a one-variable equation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Addition is the most natural elimination move; core procedural step tested in nearly every elimination problem.

in_depth.label: Elimination by subtracting equations
in_depth.description: Subtracts one equation from another to eliminate a variable with matching coefficients; targets sign errors from subtracting an entire equation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Subtraction-based elimination is a common source of sign-flip errors; tested as a distinct procedural variant.

in_depth.label: Elimination by scaling one equation
in_depth.description: Multiplies one equation by a constant so that one variable's coefficients become opposite or matching, then adds or subtracts to eliminate it.
in_depth.yield_rationale: One-equation scaling is the most common preparation step in elimination; appears at medium and high difficulty.

in_depth.label: Elimination by scaling both equations
in_depth.description: Multiplies both equations by different constants (typically using LCM) to create matching or opposite coefficients when single-equation scaling is insufficient.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Two-equation scaling is the highest-complexity elimination setup; tested at higher difficulty levels.

in_depth.label: Choosing a variable to eliminate
in_depth.description: Selects the variable whose coefficients require the smallest multipliers to align, minimizing arithmetic complexity in the elimination step.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Efficiency reasoning appears in higher-difficulty strategy questions; also implicit in every elimination solution.

umbrellas.label: Elimination Method — Execution and Special Cases
umbrellas.description: Completing the elimination method: handling negative and non-integer coefficients, solving the resulting one-variable equation, back-substitution, and detecting no-solution and dependent special cases.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Execution skills (negative coefficients, fraction/decimal variants, back-substitution) and special-case detection are required for every complete elimination solution.

in_depth.label: Elimination with negative coefficients
in_depth.description: Handles systems with negative coefficients during addition, subtraction, or scaling steps, requiring careful sign tracking throughout.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative coefficients generate the most common arithmetic errors in elimination; high distractor frequency.

in_depth.label: Elimination with fraction coefficients
in_depth.description: Applies elimination directly or after clearing fractions when equations have fraction coefficients.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fraction coefficient systems are moderately frequent; often accompanied by a clearing step.

in_depth.label: Elimination with decimal coefficients
in_depth.description: Applies elimination to systems with decimal coefficients, directly or after clearing decimals by multiplying by a power of 10.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Decimal coefficient systems appear occasionally; less frequent than fraction variants.

in_depth.label: One-variable equation created by elimination
in_depth.description: Recognizes and solves the single-variable equation that results after one variable is cancelled by elimination.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Solving the resulting one-variable equation is the central procedural payoff of elimination; exercised in every elimination problem.

in_depth.label: Back-substitution after elimination
in_depth.description: After elimination yields one variable's value, substitutes it into an original equation to find the remaining variable and complete the solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Back-substitution is required in every complete elimination solution; common site of step-omission errors.

in_depth.label: No-solution case detected by elimination
in_depth.description: Identifies an inconsistent system when elimination produces a false numeric statement (e.g., 0 = 8), signaling no solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parallel to substitution special case; both method-specific forms of inconsistency are tested.

in_depth.label: Dependent case detected by elimination
in_depth.description: Identifies a dependent system when elimination produces a true identity (e.g., 0 = 0), signaling infinitely many solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Companion to no-solution detection via elimination; both special cases appear together in classification problems.

umbrellas.label: Linear System Preparation and Structure
umbrellas.description: Rewriting system equations into forms suitable for solving, including clearing denominators and decimals, distributing, collecting like terms, rearranging variables, scaling equations, and selecting between substitution and elimination.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Preparation skills feed every algebraic solving method; method-selection reasoning appears in moderate-difficulty exam items.

in_depth.label: Standard-form linear systems
in_depth.description: Identifies and rewrites system equations in ax + by = c form, recognizing this structure as particularly suited for elimination.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Standard form is the default algebraic presentation in elimination-focused problems.

in_depth.label: Slope-intercept form linear systems
in_depth.description: Identifies and rewrites system equations in y = mx + b form, recognizing this structure as suited for graphing and direct substitution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slope-intercept form is the most common entry form for substitution-ready systems.

in_depth.label: Clearing denominators in linear systems
in_depth.description: Multiplies a system equation by the LCD of its fraction coefficients to produce an equivalent equation with integer coefficients before solving.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Clearing fractions before solving is a high-frequency preparation skill in fraction-coefficient systems.

in_depth.label: Clearing decimals in linear systems
in_depth.description: Multiplies a system equation by a power of 10 to remove decimal coefficients before applying substitution or elimination.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Decimal clearing is a useful pre-processing step; less frequent than fraction clearing.

in_depth.label: Distribution before solving systems
in_depth.description: Expands grouped expressions inside a system equation using the distributive property to align variables and constants before applying a solving method.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Distribution is required whenever system equations contain parentheses; common in moderate-difficulty problems.

in_depth.label: Like-term collection before solving systems
in_depth.description: Combines like terms within a system equation to simplify it before applying substitution or elimination.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Term collection before solving is a routine step in multi-term system equations.

in_depth.label: Rearranging variables on both sides in system equations
in_depth.description: Moves all variable terms to one side of a system equation (e.g., 2x + y = x - 3y) before applying a solving method.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Variable-on-both-sides setup appears in moderately complex system problems.

in_depth.label: Equation scaling preserves solution sets
in_depth.description: Multiplying an entire system equation by a nonzero constant produces an equivalent equation without changing the system's solution set; applies the property to justify elimination scaling.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual justification for elimination; appears in reasoning and justification question parts.

in_depth.label: Equivalent equations within systems
in_depth.description: Replaces a system equation with an equivalent simpler form (e.g., 3x - 6y = 12 becomes x - 2y = 4) without changing the system's solution set.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Simplification before solving reduces arithmetic errors; tested as a pre-processing step.

in_depth.label: Method selection: substitution versus elimination
in_depth.description: Selects between substitution (when a variable is isolated or has coefficient 1/-1) and elimination (when coefficients align or can be aligned easily) based on system structure.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Strategy selection is explicitly tested in higher-difficulty MC items and FRQ justification parts.

umbrellas.label: Special-Case and Parameter Linear Systems
umbrellas.description: Systems whose solution type depends on coefficient relationships or unknown parameters, covering proportionality conditions for parallel and coincident lines, and finding parameter values that produce each solution type.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Parameter problems require combining system reasoning with equation analysis; moderate to high difficulty; appear in advanced MC and as parts of FRQ questions in AP Precalc modeling contexts.

in_depth.label: Same slope, different intercepts — no solution
in_depth.description: Classifies a two-line system as inconsistent when both equations have equal slopes but different y-intercepts, confirming the lines are parallel.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slope-comparison route to inconsistency is among the most frequent special-case tests.

in_depth.label: Same slope, same intercept — infinitely many solutions
in_depth.description: Classifies a system as dependent when two slope-intercept equations have identical slopes and intercepts, confirming they represent the same line.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Companion classification to parallel-lines case; both appear in MC classification questions.

in_depth.label: Proportional coefficients — dependent system
in_depth.description: Identifies a dependent system in standard form when all corresponding coefficients and constants are proportional, confirming the equations represent the same line.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Coefficient-ratio check is the algebraic form of coincident-line detection; frequently tested.

in_depth.label: Proportional variable coefficients, nonproportional constants — no solution
in_depth.description: Identifies an inconsistent system when variable coefficients are proportional but constants are not, confirming parallel lines in standard form.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The coefficient-ratio inconsistency pattern is tested repeatedly because it looks similar to the dependent case.

in_depth.label: Nonproportional coefficients — one solution
in_depth.description: Identifies that nonproportional variable coefficients in a standard-form system confirm distinct non-parallel lines with exactly one intersection.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Default case for two-variable systems; recognizing it from coefficients is a prerequisite for classifying all three cases.

in_depth.label: Parameter value producing no solution
in_depth.description: Finds the value of an unknown parameter that makes the variable coefficients proportional but the constants nonproportional, forcing the system to be inconsistent.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parameter problems for inconsistency are a high-difficulty MC type; require combining proportionality reasoning with algebraic solving.

in_depth.label: Parameter value producing infinitely many solutions
in_depth.description: Finds the value of an unknown parameter that makes all corresponding coefficients and constants proportional, producing a dependent system.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The infinite-solutions parameter case is tested alongside the no-solution case; distinguishing them requires careful ratio comparison.

in_depth.label: Parameter values producing one solution
in_depth.description: Identifies all parameter values for which the system has exactly one solution by excluding values that cause parallel or coincident lines.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Complement of the no-solution and infinite-solution parameter cases; tested as a set-exclusion reasoning problem.

in_depth.label: Contradiction after system simplification
in_depth.description: Interprets a false arithmetic statement (e.g., 0 = 9) resulting from valid system transformations as proof that the system is inconsistent with no solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: False-statement interpretation is tested in both substitution and elimination special-case contexts.

in_depth.label: Identity after system simplification
in_depth.description: Interprets a true arithmetic statement (e.g., 0 = 0) resulting from valid system transformations as proof that the system is dependent with infinitely many solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: True-statement interpretation is the dependent-case complement to the contradiction case.

umbrellas.label: Three-Variable Linear Systems Without Matrices
umbrellas.description: Solving and interpreting linear systems in three variables using substitution and elimination without matrix notation, row reduction, determinants, or inverse matrices.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Three-variable systems appear rarely in AP Precalc and not at all in AP Calc AB exam items; primarily a foundations extension of two-variable methods.

in_depth.label: Three-variable system structure
in_depth.description: Recognizes a three-variable system (involving x, y, z) and identifies the number of equations and variables before choosing a solution strategy.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Structural recognition is the entry step; rarely the primary tested skill.

in_depth.label: Substitution in three-variable systems
in_depth.description: Isolates one variable in an equation and substitutes into the remaining equations to reduce a three-variable system step by step.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Substitution in three-variable systems extends two-variable logic; tested in foundation assessments.

in_depth.label: Elimination reducing three variables to two
in_depth.description: Eliminates the same variable from two pairs of equations to produce a two-variable system, the key non-matrix reduction step.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The systematic reduction step is the most procedurally complex part of three-variable work.

in_depth.label: Equation-pair selection in three-variable elimination
in_depth.description: Selects which pairs of equations to combine to eliminate a target variable, producing a solvable two-variable system.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Strategic pair selection is needed for efficiency but is embedded in the broader reduction procedure.

in_depth.label: Back-substitution in three-variable systems
in_depth.description: After solving for two variables, substitutes both values into an original equation to determine the third variable and complete the ordered triple.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Back-substitution is required for every complete three-variable solution; error-prone with three simultaneous values.

in_depth.label: Ordered-triple completion
in_depth.description: Assembles the final ordered triple (x, y, z) in the correct variable order after all three values are found.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Notation step; important for answer correctness but not a conceptually complex target.

in_depth.label: One-solution three-variable systems
in_depth.description: Classifies a three-variable system as having a unique solution when the solving process produces a single ordered triple.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Most three-variable practice problems target the one-solution case.

in_depth.label: No-solution three-variable systems
in_depth.description: Identifies a three-variable system as inconsistent when elimination or substitution produces a false numeric statement.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Special-case detection extends from two-variable systems; tested less frequently.

in_depth.label: Infinitely many solutions in three-variable systems
in_depth.description: Identifies a three-variable system as dependent when simplification produces a true identity and at least one variable is undetermined.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Dependent three-variable systems are conceptually complex and appear infrequently in foundations assessments.

in_depth.label: Free variables in dependent three-variable systems
in_depth.description: Identifies and names a free variable when a three-variable system has infinitely many solutions, expressing remaining variables as functions of the free parameter.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Free-variable parametric description is a non-matrix concept that appears rarely in foundation-level work.

umbrellas.label: Systems of Linear Inequalities — Graphing
umbrellas.description: Identifying and graphing systems of linear inequalities: boundary-line type (solid/dashed), half-plane shading direction, and the test-point method.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Graphing setup skills for inequality systems form the foundational layer before region analysis; boundary and shading conventions are the most directly tested concepts.

in_depth.label: System of linear inequalities structure
in_depth.description: Recognizes a system of linear inequalities as two or more inequalities whose solution is the set of ordered pairs satisfying all conditions simultaneously.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Definitional entry point; rarely the primary tested skill after intro level.

in_depth.label: Solution meaning for systems of inequalities
in_depth.description: Identifies that a solution to a system of inequalities is an ordered pair satisfying every inequality, corresponding to the overlap region on the coordinate plane.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Region-vs-point distinction is conceptually important for coordinate-plane inequality interpretation.

in_depth.label: Boundary lines for linear inequalities
in_depth.description: Derives the boundary line for a linear inequality by replacing the inequality symbol with an equals sign, separating the plane into two half-planes.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Boundary line identification is the first step in every inequality-graphing problem.

in_depth.label: Solid boundary lines for inclusive inequalities
in_depth.description: Uses a solid boundary line for inequalities with <= or >=, because points on the boundary are included in the solution set.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Solid/dashed distinction is a frequently tested graphical convention.

in_depth.label: Dashed boundary lines for strict inequalities
in_depth.description: Uses a dashed boundary line for inequalities with < or >, because points on the boundary are excluded from the solution set.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Paired with solid-line recognition; both graphical conventions are tested together.

in_depth.label: Shading above or below the boundary line
in_depth.description: Determines the correct half-plane to shade for an inequality solved for y: shade above for y > or y >=, below for y < or y <=.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Shading direction is the most commonly tested visual skill in inequality systems.

in_depth.label: Test-point method for determining shading
in_depth.description: Substitutes a test point (usually (0, 0) when not on the boundary) into an inequality to determine which half-plane to shade.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Test-point method is the universal fallback for shading direction when the inequality is not solved for y.

umbrellas.label: Systems of Linear Inequalities — Solution Regions
umbrellas.description: Identifying and analyzing the solution region of an inequality system: overlap regions, bounded and unbounded feasible regions, vertices, empty-overlap cases, and boundary-point inclusion.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Solution-region analysis is the application layer above graphing; vertex finding links to optimization and AP Precalc contextual modeling.

in_depth.label: Overlap region for systems of inequalities
in_depth.description: Identifies the solution region of a system as the intersection of all individual half-planes, shading only the area where every inequality is satisfied.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Overlap identification is the defining skill in graphing systems of inequalities.

in_depth.label: Bounded feasible regions
in_depth.description: Identifies a feasible region as bounded when it is enclosed on all sides by boundary lines, having finite area.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Bounded-region recognition appears in contextual inequality problems; moderate frequency.

in_depth.label: Unbounded feasible regions
in_depth.description: Identifies a feasible region as unbounded when it extends infinitely in at least one direction because the inequalities do not fully enclose the solution set.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Unbounded-region recognition is the complement to bounded; tested less frequently.

in_depth.label: Vertices of feasible regions
in_depth.description: Finds the corner points of a feasible region by solving the systems formed by pairs of intersecting boundary lines.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Vertex finding links system-solving to inequality geometry; prerequisite for optimization contexts.

in_depth.label: Systems of inequalities with no overlap
in_depth.description: Identifies when individual inequality regions have no overlap, producing a system of inequalities with no solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Empty-overlap case is a less common but important classification; tested alongside bounded/unbounded.

in_depth.label: Boundary-point inclusion in inequality systems
in_depth.description: Determines whether a point on a boundary line belongs to the solution set by checking both the line-type (solid/dashed) and whether it satisfies every inequality in the system.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Endpoint and boundary-point questions require combining solid/dashed rules with verification; a distinct reasoning step.

umbrellas.label: Nonlinear Systems — Types and Setup
umbrellas.description: Recognizing and setting up nonlinear system types: linear-quadratic, quadratic-quadratic, line-circle, and circle-circle systems, and applying substitution to reduce to a single-variable equation.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Nonlinear system types and substitution setup bridge foundations to AP Precalc function concepts; line-parabola intersections directly connect to polynomial zero analysis.

in_depth.label: Nonlinear system structure
in_depth.description: Recognizes a system as nonlinear when at least one equation is not linear (e.g., contains a squared term or a circle equation).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Structural classification is the entry step; primarily tested in intro-level nonlinear problems.

in_depth.label: Linear-quadratic systems
in_depth.description: Solves a system containing one linear and one quadratic equation by finding their intersection points, typically producing zero, one, or two solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Line-parabola intersection is the most tested nonlinear system type; connects to polynomial zero-finding and function modeling.

in_depth.label: Quadratic-quadratic systems
in_depth.description: Solves a system of two quadratic equations by setting expressions equal or using elimination to find intersection points.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parabola-parabola intersection extends linear-quadratic reasoning; moderate frequency.

in_depth.label: Line-circle systems
in_depth.description: Solves a system containing a linear equation and a circle equation (x^2 + y^2 = r^2) to find intersection points, yielding zero, one, or two solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Line-circle systems appear in foundations and connect to coordinate geometry.

in_depth.label: Circle-circle systems
in_depth.description: Solves a system of two circle equations to find shared intersection points, if any exist.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Circle-circle intersection is the least common nonlinear system type in foundations assessments.

in_depth.label: Substitution in nonlinear systems
in_depth.description: Substitutes one expression from a nonlinear system into the other equation, producing a single-variable nonlinear equation to solve.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Substitution is the primary technique for nonlinear systems; widely tested across all nonlinear system types.

umbrellas.label: Nonlinear Systems — Solution Analysis
umbrellas.description: Analyzing nonlinear system solutions: reducing to a quadratic equation, counting real solutions, identifying tangency, verifying candidates, and rejecting extraneous roots.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Solution analysis skills for nonlinear systems connect to discriminant reasoning, graphical intersection counting, and extraneous-root handling in AP Precalc function-model contexts.

in_depth.label: Nonlinear systems reducing to a quadratic equation
in_depth.description: Recognizes that most nonlinear system substitutions produce a quadratic equation as the final single-variable equation to solve.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Quadratic reduction is the most common intermediate step in nonlinear system solving; connects to factoring and quadratic formula.

in_depth.label: Number of real solutions in nonlinear systems
in_depth.description: Determines whether a nonlinear system has zero, one, two, or more real solutions from algebraic discriminant analysis or graphical intersection count.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Solution-count reasoning connects to discriminant analysis of quadratics and is tested in both algebraic and graphical forms.

in_depth.label: Tangent intersection in nonlinear systems
in_depth.description: Identifies the special case where a line or curve is tangent to another curve, producing exactly one intersection point (a repeated root of the resulting quadratic).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Tangency as one-solution case links the discriminant equal to zero with the geometric concept of tangency.

in_depth.label: Solution verification in nonlinear systems
in_depth.description: Checks candidate solutions by substituting into every equation, which is especially important in nonlinear systems where multiple candidates may arise.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Verification after solving is a required step when the quadratic produces two candidates; both must be checked.

in_depth.label: Extraneous candidates in nonlinear systems
in_depth.description: Identifies and rejects candidate solutions that fail to satisfy one or more of the original system equations after the algebraic solving process.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Extraneous solutions appear when squaring or other nonlinear operations introduce spurious roots; tested in higher-difficulty problems.

in_depth.label: Graphical intersections in nonlinear systems
in_depth.description: Reads or estimates intersection points between nonlinear curves from a graph, connecting geometric intersection to algebraic system solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical interpretation of nonlinear solutions reinforces the algebraic-graphical connection tested in AP Precalc representation questions.

umbrellas.label: Structured Linear System Relationships
umbrellas.description: Common algebraic relationship patterns — sum-difference, total-part, value-quantity, rate-total, mixture, comparison, break-even, and two-constraint structures — that naturally and efficiently form two-variable linear systems.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Pattern-to-system translation is directly exercised in AP Precalc function-model construction (1.14, 2.5, 3.7) when two conditions define a system for parameter extraction.

in_depth.label: Sum-and-difference systems
in_depth.description: Translates 'sum is S and difference is D' into a 2x2 system (x + y = S, x - y = D) that solves efficiently by adding or subtracting equations.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sum-difference is the most natural and frequently occurring structured system pattern.

in_depth.label: Total-and-part systems
in_depth.description: Writes a system using one equation for total quantity (x + y = T) and one for a relationship between the parts (e.g., x = k*y), then solves for each part.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Part-total structure underlies many two-condition function-model problems.

in_depth.label: Value-and-quantity systems
in_depth.description: Forms a system with one equation for total quantity (x + y = n) and one for total value (v1*x + v2*y = V) when two item types have different unit values.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Value-quantity systems appear in two-condition model construction; the structure is reused in exponential/linear parameter fitting.

in_depth.label: Rate-and-total systems
in_depth.description: Forms a system where two quantities are related by a total-quantity equation and a weighted-rate equation (r1*x + r2*y = total), solving for unknown amounts.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rate-total structure generalizes value-quantity; both are common in AP Precalc modeling.

in_depth.label: Two-component mixture systems
in_depth.description: Writes a system using one equation for total amount and one for total weighted contribution (concentration, value, etc.) in a two-component mixture.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Mixture systems are a classic two-condition structure; moderate frequency in foundations assessments.

in_depth.label: Comparison systems
in_depth.description: Identifies a system containing a direct comparison equation (x = y + k or x = k*y) as a substitution-ready structure and solves for both quantities.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Comparison structure is common in all contexts where one quantity is expressed relative to another.

in_depth.label: Break-even systems
in_depth.description: Finds the input value where two linear expressions are equal (e.g., cost = revenue) by writing and solving a system or by setting expressions equal.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Break-even is a canonical two-linear-function comparison problem; appears in function-model contexts.

in_depth.label: Linear systems from two simultaneous constraints
in_depth.description: Translates two independent algebraic constraints on the same variables into a linear system and identifies which constraint forms each equation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Two-constraint translation is the general form of all structured system patterns; directly mirrors AP Precalc parameter-extraction tasks.

in_depth.label: Writing a linear system from contextual conditions
in_depth.description: Translates a multi-condition contextual description into a two-variable linear system by assigning variables and writing one equation per condition.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Model-construction from context is directly tested in AP Precalc FRQ 2 and in function-parameter extraction tasks (1.14, 2.5, 3.7).
